metadata.summary: Run controlled experiments comparing two variants against quantitative metrics to validate design changes with statistical confidence before shipping.

content.title: A/B Testing
content.summary: Design decisions based on opinions create debates without resolution. A/B testing (controlled experimentation) compares a control version against a variant by randomly assigning users to each, measuring conversion or engagement impact, and determining statistical significance. Use when you have a specific metric to measure, enough traffic to reach significance within a reasonable timeframe, and need to validate that a change does not harm secondary or guardrail metrics.

why.p: A/B tests replace opinion-based design debates with quantitative data. Instead of arguing whether a larger button or different copy increases conversions, the test measures the actual impact. The statistical framework determines whether observed differences are real or random noise.

The structured methodology (hypothesis → sample size calculation → run → analyze → decide) forces discipline: teams write success criteria before implementation, preventing post-hoc rationalization of results. Statistical significance must be reached at the pre-calculated sample size; stopping early ("looks good") introduces p-hacking bias.

list.item: You have a specific, measurable conversion metric (sign-ups, click-through, checkout completion) and want to validate a design change improves it.
A design debate cannot be resolved by expert judgment and a quantitative answer is needed.
You have enough traffic to reach statistical significance within a reasonable run time (typically 1-2 weeks).
You are iterating on a proven flow to optimize incrementally—not when redesigning from scratch.
You want to validate that a change helps the primary metric without harming secondary or guardrail metrics.
On low-traffic pages where reaching statistical significance would take months or years—qualitative research is faster.
During major product redesigns where user mental models differ—test user understanding first with qualitative research (interviews, usability testing).
When you need to understand why users behave differently, not just whether they do—use session recordings and interviews instead.
For complex multi-step flows where the winning variant on step 1 may harm completion on step 5—funnel analysis with holdout groups is needed.
On pages with high seasonal variance without adjusting for the cycle.

how-to.step: Form a hypothesis using the template: If we [change], then [metric] will [improve/decrease] by [amount] because [reason]. Write it before any implementation.
Define three metric sets: primary (the main thing being measured), secondary (other impacts to watch), and guardrail metrics (things that should not worsen).

Calculate required sample size using baseline conversion rate, minimum detectable effect (MDE), statistical power (typically 80%), and significance level (typically 95%). Use a sample size calculator or statistical library to determine how many users per variant are needed.
Implement the test: set up random user assignment (fully randomized, never sticky), ensure consistent experience per user, verify tracking accuracy, and commit to a fixed run duration.
Run the test for at least one full business cycle (minimum 7-14 days) to account for day-of-week effects, regardless of when statistical significance appears.

After the pre-calculated sample size is reached, analyze results: check statistical significance, examine practical significance, analyze key segments (new vs. returning, mobile vs. desktop), and verify guardrail metrics.
Make a decision: Clear winner = implement variant. No difference = keep original (simpler). Inconclusive = extend test or reject. Negative impact = stop test and investigate.

section.name: key-concepts
section.p: Understanding the terminology is essential to avoid common mistakes.
section.term: The current version of the page or feature being tested.
The new version with one or more changes.
The desired user action (sign-up, click, purchase, completion).
A result is statistically significant at 95% confidence if there is only a 5% chance the observed difference occurred by random chance. More formally, the p-value is less than 0.05.
The number of users needed in each variant to detect the minimum detectable effect (MDE) with the desired confidence level and power.
The smallest relative improvement you want to detect (e.g., 10% relative lift on a baseline 2% conversion rate = target of 2.2%).
The probability of detecting the MDE if it truly exists. Typically set to 80%—meaning a 20% chance of missing a true effect.
The probability that the observed difference occurred by random chance. A p-value < 0.05 indicates statistical significance at 95% confidence.

section.name: process-detail
section.p: Each step in the structured process serves a specific purpose in producing reliable, actionable results.

rule.statement: Write the hypothesis and success criteria before implementation. Commit to the primary metric and acceptable trade-offs on secondary metrics in writing.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc metric selection inflates false positive rates. Teams unconsciously add metrics after seeing results, turning the test into a fishing expedition. Pre-commitment prevents this.

rule.statement: Run for at least one full business cycle (7-14 days minimum) regardless of when statistical significance is reached. Do not stop early just because results look promising.
rule.rationale: Early stopping is p-hacking. Day-of-week effects are real: Monday visitors behave differently than Friday visitors. Weekend vs. weekday traffic patterns differ. Running fewer than 7 days captures only a partial cycle and produces fragile results.

rule.statement: Check sample ratio mismatch (SRM) first: if the control and variant have significantly different sample sizes despite a 50/50 split, the randomization is broken and the entire test is invalid.
rule.rationale: SRM indicates that something is wrong with the assignment mechanism (sticky cookie, bot exclusion rules applied unequally, user dropout tied to variant). A test with SRM cannot be trusted.

rule.statement: Always analyze key segments before concluding (new vs. returning, mobile vs. desktop, geography if relevant). Aggregate results can hide important reversals: desktop users prefer variant A while mobile users prefer variant B.
rule.rationale: Simpson's paradox: aggregate results point one direction while segment-level results point another. Shipping the aggregate winner may harm the largest segment. Segment analysis surfaces these conflicts.

example.title: CTA Button Test with Guardrails
example.code:
Hypothesis: Changing the CTA button from "Submit" to "Get Started Free" with green background will increase click-through rate by at least 15% because it is more action-oriented and visually prominent.
Primary metric: CTA click-through rate (baseline 2.3%) Secondary metrics: Time on page, scroll depth Guardrail metrics: Bounce rate, unsubscribe rate, support tickets
Sample size needed: 2,500 per variant (95% confidence, 80% power, detecting 15% lift) Expected run time: 10 days at 500 daily visitors
Results after 5 days: 3.1% CTR in variant (35% lift). STOP — do not decide yet. Run full 10 days.
Final results: Variant 3.1% (n=2,500), Control 2.3% (n=2,500), p-value 0.032, 95% CI [0.0015, 0.0185]. Guardrails: Bounce rate Control 15.2% vs Variant 15.1% (no change). Unsubscribe Control 0.8% vs Variant 0.8% (no change). Segments: New users: variant 3.5% vs control 2.2% (60% lift). Returning users: variant 2.9% vs control 2.4% (21% lift). Decision: Ship variant. All segments prefer it; guardrails unaffected.

example.title: No Clear Winner—Keep Control
example.code:
Test: One-column checkout vs. two-column checkout
Results: One-column 68.2% completion, Two-column 69.1% completion (0.9 percentage point difference). Statistical significance: Not significant at 95% confidence. The confidence interval spans zero.
Decision: Keep one-column (control). It is simpler to maintain and produces no worse result. The 0.9 point difference, even if real, is smaller than the operational cost of maintaining two versions.

antipattern.description: Stopping the test early when results look good. "We reached significance on day 4, so we shipped."
antipattern.reason: Early stopping is p-hacking. Sequential testing inflates false positive rates unless using sequential analysis corrections (Pocock or O'Brien-Fleming boundaries). The pre-calculated sample size accounts for day-of-week effects. Stopping early misses them.

antipattern.description: Running too many variants at once. "Let us test button color (3 options), size (2 options), and copy (2 options) simultaneously." That is 12 combinations.
antipattern.reason: Multivariate testing requires exponentially larger sample sizes. 12 combinations need 12x the traffic to detect the same effect size as a single A/B test. Run sequential tests instead: narrow down button color first, then test size on the winning color.

antipattern.description: Ignoring the novelty effect. "Variant got 25% lift in week 1; ship it!"
antipattern.reason: New UI elements get initial clicks simply because they are new. The lift often decays after week 1 as novelty wears off. The test duration must be long enough to observe the decay if it exists.

antipattern.description: Confusing statistical significance with practical significance. "The result is significant at p=0.04, so it is important."
antipattern.reason: Statistical significance says the effect is real, not that it matters. A 0.1% lift at 99% confidence may not be worth implementing if the engineering cost is high. Always check both the confidence interval and the practical magnitude.

section.name: multivariate-testing
section.p: Testing multiple changes simultaneously is tempting but requires careful statistical handling.

rule.statement: For multivariate tests (2+ changes), either use a factorial design with sufficient sample size to detect interactions, or run sequential tests (test one variable, hold winner, test next variable).
rule.rationale: Factorial designs require exponentially larger sample sizes. Sequential testing is faster but may miss interactions (color + size may have a joint effect that neither has alone). Choose based on traffic volume.

rule.statement: When testing multiple variables or multiple primary metrics, apply multiple comparison correction (Bonferroni or false discovery rate control) to adjust the significance threshold.
rule.rationale: Testing 10 metrics at 95% confidence gives a 40% false positive rate on at least one metric. Correction maintains family-wise error rate.

section.name: templates-and-examples
section.p: Structured templates keep documentation consistent across tests. Templates cover the A/B test plan (design phase) and results report (analysis phase).

rule.statement: Use standardized plan and results templates for every test to ensure the same information is captured consistently.
rule.rationale: Standardized templates enable comparison across tests over time. Ad-hoc documentation prevents learning from past tests and makes it hard to assess whether test maturity is improving.

section.name: common-statistical-mistakes
section.p: Statistical errors in test execution and interpretation are common and can lead to shipping ineffective variants or failing to ship improvements.

antipattern.description: Novelty effect: new UI elements get clicks simply because they are new; watch for lift that decays after week 1.
antipattern.reason: Participants or users see something new and engage more, but the lift is temporary. Running tests for at least one full business cycle reveals whether novelty effects decay or persist.

antipattern.description: Simpson's paradox: aggregate results can hide segment reversals. Desktop users prefer variant A, mobile users prefer variant B.
antipattern.reason: The overall winner may not be the best variant for all segments. Always analyze key segments before concluding.

antipattern.description: Interaction effects: running two simultaneous tests on overlapping user populations creates confounding.
antipattern.reason: If variant A is running on feature X and variant B is running on feature Y, and the same users see both, you cannot isolate which variant drove the result. Randomize users across tests to prevent overlaps or stagger tests.

antipattern.description: Statistical significance is not practical significance: a 0.1% lift at 99% confidence may not be worth implementing if engineering cost is high.
antipattern.reason: Always check both the confidence interval (is the effect real?) and the practical magnitude (is it worth doing?). Statistical significance answers the first question only.

antipattern.description: Tests run too short surface false positives; stopping at "significance" without reaching pre-calculated sample size is p-hacking.
antipattern.reason: The pre-calculated sample size accounts for day-of-week effects, business cycles, and traffic variation. Stopping early misses these effects and inflates false positive rates.

section.name: verification-checklist
section.p: Use this checklist before launching a test and after results arrive.

checklist.check: Hypothesis is written in the template format and approved before implementation.
Primary, secondary, and guardrail metrics are defined in writing.
Sample size is calculated based on baseline rate, MDE, 80% power, and 95% significance.
Random assignment is verified (cookie assignment logs checked for randomness).
Tracking is verified on both control and variant (test conversions on staging before live).
Test runs for the full pre-calculated duration or until sample size is reached (whichever is later).
Sample ratio mismatch is checked first (if control and variant have different n despite 50/50 split, randomization is broken).
Statistical significance is calculated and reported as p-value and confidence interval, not just yes/no.
Key segments are analyzed (new vs. returning, mobile vs. desktop, etc.).
Guardrail metrics are checked for regression (did the variant harm secondary metrics?).
Decision is documented (ship, don't ship, extend, investigate) with rationale.
Learnings from the test (including null and negative results) are documented and shared.

template.purpose: Test plan skeleton: hypothesis, design, metrics, sample size, timeline, risks.
Results report: summary, metrics table, statistical details, segment analysis, insights, next steps.
